title: Periarticular Disorders of the Extremities
section: Disorders of the Joints and Adjacent Tissues

items.subtopic: Periarticular Disorders of the Extremities | BURSITIS
items.title: Common Causes and Presentation of Subacromial Bursitis
items.content: Subacromial bursitis is frequently associated with rotator cuff tendinitis, presenting as pain in the anterolateral shoulder. It's crucial to differentiate it from other shoulder disorders through directed examination and imaging.
keyPoints: Most common form of bursitis
Often accompanies rotator cuff tendinitis
Pain in the anterolateral aspect of the shoulder
Imaging important for differential diagnosis
items.reference: Periarticular Disorders of the Extremities · Periarticular Disorders of the Extremities | BURSITIS

items.subtopic: Periarticular Disorders of the Extremities | BURSITIS
items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Olecranon Bursitis
items.content: Olecranon bursitis presents with visible swelling over the elbow. Acute inflammation with redness or warmth necessitates aspiration for Gram stain, culture, and urate crystal examination to exclude infection or gout.
keyPoints: Involves posterior elbow with possible visible swelling
Aspiration needed if acute inflammation is present
Gram stain, culture, and urate crystals examined to rule out infection or gout
items.reference: Periarticular Disorders of the Extremities · Periarticular Disorders of the Extremities | BURSITIS

items.subtopic: Periarticular Disorders of the Extremities | BURSITIS
items.title: Characteristics and Treatment of Trochanteric Bursitis
items.content: Trochanteric bursitis, often with gluteus medius/minimus tendinopathy, causes lateral hip pain. Pain is elicited by external rotation, resisted abduction, or direct pressure over the posterior greater trochanter.
keyPoints: Pain over lateral hip and upper thigh
Tenderness over posterior greater trochanter
Pain with external rotation, resisted abduction, or direct pressure
items.reference: Periarticular Disorders of the Extremities · Periarticular Disorders of the Extremities | BURSITIS

items.subtopic: ROTATOR CUFF TENDINITIS AND IMPINGEMENT SYNDROME | ROTATOR CUFF TEARS
items.title: Understanding Rotator Cuff Tendinitis and Impingement Syndrome: Mechanisms and Management
items.content: Rotator cuff tendinitis, often referred to as impingement syndrome, is a prevalent cause of shoulder pain. It primarily involves the supraspinatus tendon due to its susceptibility to impingement between the humeral head and the acromion or coracoacromial ligament. Symptoms typically include dull ache exacerbated by arm elevation in specific arcs (60° to 120°). Management often starts with NSAIDs, glucocorticoid injections, and physical therapy, progressing to surgical intervention if conservative measures fail.
keyPoints: The supraspinatus tendon is most commonly affected due to impingement risk.
Symptoms include a dull ache worsened by arm elevation between 60° and 120°.
Initial management includes NSAIDs, glucocorticoid injections, and physical therapy.
Surgical decompression may be necessary for refractory cases.
items.reference: Periarticular Disorders of the Extremities · ROTATOR CUFF TENDINITIS AND IMPINGEMENT SYNDROME | ROTATOR CUFF TEARS

items.subtopic: ROTATOR CUFF TENDINITIS AND IMPINGEMENT SYNDROME | ROTATOR CUFF TEARS
items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Rotator Cuff Tears: Clinical Approach
items.content: Rotator cuff tears can be acute, subacute, or chronic, with varying etiologies. Symptoms include pain and weakness in shoulder abduction and external rotation. Diagnosis is suggested by specific physical exam maneuvers like a painful abduction arc above 90° and confirmed via ultrasound or MRI. Management depends on factors such as tear severity, symptom duration, and patient characteristics, with surgical referral recommended for moderate to severe tears causing functional loss.
keyPoints: Rotator cuff tears can be acute, subacute, or chronic.
Symptoms include pain and weakness in shoulder abduction and external rotation.
Diagnosis involves physical exam maneuvers and imaging (ultrasound/MRI).
Surgical referral is indicated for moderate to severe tears with functional loss.
items.reference: Periarticular Disorders of the Extremities · ROTATOR CUFF TENDINITIS AND IMPINGEMENT SYNDROME | ROTATOR CUFF TEARS

items.subtopic: CALCIFIC TENDINITIS | BICIPITAL TENDINITIS AND RUPTURE
items.title: Understanding Calcific Tendinitis: Diagnosis and Management
items.content: Calcific tendinitis involves calcium salt deposition in tendons, predominantly affecting the shoulder's rotator cuff. The supraspinatus tendon is involved in 63% of cases. It typically manifests after age 40 and may be linked to diabetes, thyroid disorders, or genetic factors. Diagnosis can be confirmed via radiograph or ultrasound. Most cases resolve with conservative treatment like physical therapy and NSAIDs, but persistent pain might require interventions such as needle aspiration, shock wave therapy, or surgery.
keyPoints: Calcium salts deposition primarily in the shoulder's rotator cuff tendons
Supraspinatus tendon involvement in 63% of patients
Typically develops after age 40
Diagnosis via radiograph or ultrasound
Conservative treatment includes physical therapy and NSAIDs
Refractory cases may need ultrasound-guided needle aspiration, shock wave therapy, or surgery
items.reference: Periarticular Disorders of the Extremities · CALCIFIC TENDINITIS | BICIPITAL TENDINITIS AND RUPTURE

items.subtopic: CALCIFIC TENDINITIS | BICIPITAL TENDINITIS AND RUPTURE
items.title: Bicipital Tendinitis and Rupture: Clinical Presentation and Management
items.content: Bicipital tendinitis results from friction in the biceps tendon within the bicipital groove, causing anterior shoulder pain that radiates down to the forearm. Pain is exacerbated by arm abduction, external rotation, or resisting supination (Yergason's test). Acute rupture can occur during vigorous exercise and requires prompt surgical repair in active individuals. In older patients, ruptures may be less painful and are often managed conservatively due to tendon retraction.
keyPoints: Friction on the biceps tendon within the bicipital groove causes tendinitis
Pain radiates from shoulder to forearm, worsened by specific movements
Yergason's test can elicit pain along the tendon
Acute rupture in active patients requires surgical repair
Older patient ruptures may be asymptomatic and managed conservatively
items.reference: Periarticular Disorders of the Extremities · CALCIFIC TENDINITIS | BICIPITAL TENDINITIS AND RUPTURE

items.subtopic: DE QUERVAIN'S TENOSYNOVITIS
items.title: Diagnosis and Initial Management of De Quervain's Tenosynovitis
items.content: De Quervain's tenosynovitis is characterized by inflammation of the abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis tendons. It typically results from repetitive wrist twisting, pregnancy, or certain infant-holding positions. Diagnosis can be confirmed with a positive Finkelstein test, which elicits pain over the radial styloid process when the thumb is ulnar deviated in the palm.
keyPoints: Inflammation of abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis tendons
Common causes: repetitive wrist twisting, pregnancy, infant-holding positions
Symptoms: pain on grasping or pinching with thumb, swelling, and tenderness over radial styloid
Diagnostic test: positive Finkelstein test
Initial treatment: wrist splinting and NSAIDs; severe cases may require local glucocorticoid injections
items.reference: Periarticular Disorders of the Extremities · DE QUERVAIN'S TENOSYNOVITIS | PATELLAR TENDINITIS | DRUG-INDUCED TENDINOPATHIES

items.subtopic: DRUG-INDUCED TENDINOPATHIES
items.title: Recognition and Management of Drug-Induced Tendinopathies
items.content: Drug-induced tendinopathies are increasingly recognized with the use of certain pharmacologic agents. The most associated drug classes include quinolones, glucocorticoids, aromatase inhibitors, and statins, often affecting lower extremity tendons like the Achilles tendon. Diagnosis may involve ultrasound or MRI to assess tendon structure and integrity.
keyPoints: Associated drugs: quinolones, glucocorticoids, aromatase inhibitors, statins
Commonly affected tendon: Achilles tendon
Symptoms: pain, swelling over the tendon, possible tendon rupture
Diagnostic tools: ultrasound, MRI for tendon assessment
Management: discontinue suspected drug; surgical intervention may be needed for ruptures
items.reference: Periarticular Disorders of the Extremities · DE QUERVAIN'S TENOSYNOVITIS | PATELLAR TENDINITIS | DRUG-INDUCED TENDINOPATHIES

items.subtopic: ILIOTIBIAL BAND SYNDROME | ADHESIVE CAPSULITIS
items.title: Management Strategies for Iliotibial Band Syndrome
items.content: Iliotibial band syndrome is managed through a combination of rest, NSAIDs, physical therapy, and addressing modifiable risk factors. For persistent symptoms, glucocorticoid injections can be used, but patients must avoid running for at least 2 weeks post-injection to ensure recovery.
keyPoints: Pain occurs where the iliotibial band crosses the lateral femoral condyle of the knee.
Risk factors include varus alignment, distance running, poorly fitted shoes, and uneven terrain.
Treatment includes rest, NSAIDs, physical therapy, and addressing risk factors.
Glucocorticoid injection can provide relief; avoid running for 2 weeks post-injection.
Surgical release is considered in rare cases where conservative treatment fails.
items.reference: Periarticular Disorders of the Extremities · ILIOTIBIAL BAND SYNDROME | ADHESIVE CAPSULITIS

items.subtopic: ILIOTIBIAL BAND SYNDROME | ADHESIVE CAPSULITIS
items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Adhesive Capsulitis
items.content: Adhesive capsulitis, or frozen shoulder, is diagnosed based on physical examination showing reduced range of motion not due to other causes. It's associated with systemic disorders like diabetes and occurs more commonly in women over age 50. Treatment focuses on early mobilization, physical therapy, NSAIDs, glucocorticoid injections, and possibly manipulation under anesthesia.
keyPoints: Characterized by pain and restricted shoulder movement without intrinsic disease.
More common in women over age 50; associated with systemic disorders.
Diagnosis is clinical: reduced range of motion on physical examination.
No specific findings on plain radiograph, ultrasound, or MRI.
Treatment includes early mobilization, physical therapy, NSAIDs, glucocorticoid injections, and manipulation under anesthesia.
items.reference: Periarticular Disorders of the Extremities · ILIOTIBIAL BAND SYNDROME | ADHESIVE CAPSULITIS

items.subtopic: LATERAL EPICONDYLITIS | MEDIAL EPICONDYLITIS
items.title: Management of Lateral Epicondylitis (Tennis Elbow)
items.content: Lateral epicondylitis, or tennis elbow, involves pain at the lateral aspect of the elbow due to overuse. It is managed initially with rest and NSAIDs, avoiding aggravating activities. Additional treatments include ultrasound, icing, friction massage, and a forearm band placed 2.5–5.0 cm below the elbow. Severe cases may benefit from a one-time local glucocorticoid injection, but repeat injections should be avoided. After 4-6 weeks of rest, physical therapy is recommended to strengthen extensor muscles.
keyPoints: Pain originates near the lateral epicondyle and radiates into the forearm and wrist.
Common activities causing injury include pulling weeds, carrying heavy items, or using a screwdriver.
Treatment involves rest, NSAIDs, ultrasound, icing, friction massage, and possibly a one-time glucocorticoid injection for severe pain.
After 4-6 weeks of rest, begin physical therapy to strengthen extensor muscles.
A forearm band placed 2.5–5.0 cm below the elbow can reduce tension on extensor muscles.
items.reference: Periarticular Disorders of the Extremities · LATERAL EPICONDYLITIS | MEDIAL EPICONDYLITIS

items.subtopic: LATERAL EPICONDYLITIS | MEDIAL EPICONDYLITIS
items.title: Management of Medial Epicondylitis (Golfer's Elbow)
items.content: Medial epicondylitis, or golfer's elbow, results from repetitive wrist flexion and pronation leading to pain over the medial elbow. Initial treatment is conservative with rest, NSAIDs, friction massage, ultrasound, and icing. For severe pain, a one-time local glucocorticoid injection may be considered. After 4-6 weeks of rest, physical therapy should follow once pain subsides. In chronic cases unresponsive to at least a year of treatment, surgical release of the flexor muscle origin may be necessary.
keyPoints: Pain is due to microtears in pronator teres and forearm flexors from repetitive wrist flexion and pronation.
Commonly seen in patients over 35 years old, often related to work or recreational activities like golfing or baseball.
Initial treatment includes rest, NSAIDs, friction massage, ultrasound, and icing.
Severe pain may be managed with a one-time local glucocorticoid injection.
After 4-6 weeks of rest, initiate physical therapy once pain subsides.
Surgical release may be necessary for chronic cases unresponsive to treatment after at least a year.
items.reference: Periarticular Disorders of the Extremities · LATERAL EPICONDYLITIS | MEDIAL EPICONDYLITIS

items.subtopic: PLANTAR FASCIITIS
items.title: Diagnosis and Initial Management of Plantar Fasciitis
items.content: Plantar fasciitis is typically diagnosed based on clinical history and physical examination. Patients often report severe pain with the first steps in the morning or after periods of inactivity, which lessens with activity but worsens with prolonged use. Physical exam reveals tenderness at the plantar fascia's calcaneal attachment. Initial management includes conservative measures such as ice, heat, massage, stretching, and avoiding aggravating activities.
keyPoints: Pain is severe in the morning or after inactivity, lessening with activity but worsening over time.
Tenderness at the plantar fascia's calcaneal attachment site on palpation.
>80% of patients resolve symptoms within 12 months with conservative treatment.
Initial treatments: ice, heat, massage, stretching, and avoiding inciting activities.
items.reference: Periarticular Disorders of the Extremities · PLANTAR FASCIITIS

items.subtopic: PLANTAR FASCIITIS
items.title: Indications for Imaging and Advanced Treatment in Plantar Fasciitis
items.content: Imaging is generally not required to diagnose plantar fasciitis but may be used when the diagnosis is unclear. Plain radiographs can show heel spurs, which are not diagnostically significant. Ultrasonography shows fascia thickening and edema, while MRI is sensitive but typically unnecessary for diagnosis. Advanced treatments like local glucocorticoid injections or plantar fasciotomy are considered after 6-12 months of unsuccessful conservative management.
keyPoints: Imaging not usually needed; used when diagnosis unclear.
Radiographs may show heel spurs, which lack diagnostic significance.
Ultrasonography shows fascia thickening and edema at the calcaneal attachment.
MRI is sensitive but generally unnecessary for initial diagnosis.
Advanced treatments (injections or surgery) after 6-12 months of failed conservative treatment.
items.reference: Periarticular Disorders of the Extremities · PLANTAR FASCIITIS

items.subtopic: ROTATOR CUFF TENDINITIS AND IMPINGEMENT SYNDROME | ROTATOR CUFF TEARS
items.assertion: Surgical decompression may be necessary in patients refractory to conservative treatment for rotator cuff tendinitis.
items.reason: Conservative treatments like NSAIDs, local glucocorticoid injection, and physical therapy can relieve symptoms.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; however, the reason does not explain why surgery might be necessary.
items.reference: Periarticular Disorders of the Extremities · ROTATOR CUFF TENDINITIS AND IMPINGEMENT SYNDROME | ROTATOR CUFF TEARS

items.subtopic: CALCIFIC TENDINITIS | BICIPITAL TENDINITIS AND RUPTURE
items.assertion: Calcific tendinitis often presents with sudden and severe shoulder pain.
items.reason: Calcification within the tendon may evoke acute inflammation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Periarticular Disorders of the Extremities · CALCIFIC TENDINITIS | BICIPITAL TENDINITIS AND RUPTURE
